title: Mayor–council government

infobox.key: Executive and legislature [fused](/wiki/Fusion_of_powers "Fusion of powers")  [City commission](/wiki/City_commission_government "City commission government")
infobox.value: | [Direct democratic forms](/wiki/Direct_democracy "Direct democracy")  * [Town meeting](/wiki/Town_meeting "Town meeting")   + [Representative town meeting](/wiki/Representative_town_meeting "Representative town meeting")

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Local_government_forms "Template:Local government forms") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Local_government_forms "Template talk:Local government forms") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Local_government_forms "Special:EditPage/Template:Local government forms")

is a system of [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") in which a [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") who is directly elected by the voters acts as chief executive, while a separately elected [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council") constitutes the legislative body. It is one of the two most common forms of [local government in the United States](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_United_States "Local government in the United States"), and is the form most frequently adopted in large cities, although the other common form, [council–manager government](/wiki/Council%E2%80%93manager_government "Council–manager government"), is the [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") form of more municipalities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Kathy Hayes; Semoon Chang (July 1990). "The Relative Efficiency of City Manager and Mayor–Council Forms of Government". *Southern Economic Journal*. **57** (1): 167–177. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.2307/1060487](https://doi.org/10.2307%2F1060487). [JSTOR](/wiki/JSTOR_(identifier) "JSTOR (identifier)") [1060487](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1060487).

Saffell, Dave C.; Harry Basehart (2009). *State and Local Government: Politics and Public Policies* (9th ed.). McGraw Hill. p. 237. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-07-352632-4](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-07-352632-4 "Special:BookSources/978-0-07-352632-4").

George C. Edwards III; Robert L. Lineberry; Martin P. Wattenberg (2006). *Government in America*. Pearson Education. pp. 677–678. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-321-29236-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-321-29236-7 "Special:BookSources/0-321-29236-7").

[*Decentralization and Local Democracy in the World: First Global Report by United Cities and Local Governments 2008*](https://books.google.com/books?id=8m2CQeX4EuwC&dq=mayor+council+system+in+asia&pg=PA80). World Bank Publications. 4 November 2008. pp. 80–81. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-8213-7735-2](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-8213-7735-2 "Special:BookSources/978-0-8213-7735-2").

Benjamin (senior researcher, Japan Local Government Center), Seth B.; Grant (director, ICMA), Jason (29 March 2022). ["Japan: Local Autonomy Is a Central Tenet to Good Governance"](https://icma.org/articles/article/japan-local-autonomy-central-tenet-good-governance). [International City/County Management Association](/wiki/International_City/County_Management_Association "International City/County Management Association") (ICMA). Retrieved 30 July 2025.

Colin Copus (26 May 2006). ["Chapter 7 (*The elected mayor: lessons from overseas*)"](https://academic.oup.com/manchester-scholarship-online/book/19882/chapter-abstract/178776330?redirectedFrom=fulltext). *Leading the Localities: Executive Mayors in English Local Governance*. Manchester University Press. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.7228/manchester/9780719071867.003.0007](https://doi.org/10.7228%2Fmanchester%2F9780719071867.003.0007). [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780719071867](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780719071867 "Special:BookSources/9780719071867"). ([registration required](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Access_to_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")) (online date= 19 July 2012, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-1-78170-137-9](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-78170-137-9 "Special:BookSources/978-1-78170-137-9"))

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Form of local government

|  |
| --- |
| Part of the [Politics series](/wiki/Category:Politics "Category:Politics") |
| Common forms of [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") |
| Executive and legislature [separate](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers")  * Mayor–council * [Council–manager](/wiki/Council%E2%80%93manager_government "Council–manager government") |
| Executive and legislature [fused](/wiki/Fusion_of_powers "Fusion of powers")  [City commission](/wiki/City_commission_government "City commission government") |
| [Direct democratic forms](/wiki/Direct_democracy "Direct democracy")  * [Town meeting](/wiki/Town_meeting "Town meeting")   + [Representative town meeting](/wiki/Representative_town_meeting "Representative town meeting") |
| [icon](/wiki/File:A_coloured_voting_box.svg) [Politics portal](/wiki/Portal:Politics "Portal:Politics") |
| * [v](/wiki/Template:Local_government_forms "Template:Local government forms") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Local_government_forms "Template talk:Local government forms") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Local_government_forms "Special:EditPage/Template:Local government forms") |

A **mayor–council government** is a system of [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") in which a [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") who is directly elected by the voters acts as chief executive, while a separately elected [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council") constitutes the legislative body. It is one of the two most common forms of [local government in the United States](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_United_States "Local government in the United States"), and is the form most frequently adopted in large cities, although the other common form, [council–manager government](/wiki/Council%E2%80%93manager_government "Council–manager government"), is the [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") form of more municipalities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Variations and mayoral power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mayor%E2%80%93council_government&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Variations and mayoral power")]

The form may be categorized into two main variations depending on the relative power of the mayor compared to the council, the *strong-mayor* variant and the *weak-mayor* variant.

In a typical *strong-mayor* system, the elected mayor is granted almost total administrative authority with the power to appoint and dismiss department heads, although some city charters or prevailing state law may require council ratification. In such a system, the mayor's administrative staff often prepares the city budget, although that budget must be approved by the council.[[1]](#cite_note-JSOTR-1) The mayor may also have veto rights over council votes, with the council able to override such a veto.

Conversely, in a *weak-mayor* system, the mayor has no formal authority outside the council, serving a largely ceremonial role as council chairperson and is elected by the citizens of the city. The mayor cannot directly appoint or remove officials and lacks veto power over council votes.[[2]](#cite_note-saffell-2)

## Use across the world

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mayor%E2%80%93council_government&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Use across the world")]

Most major North American cities use the strong-mayor form of the mayor–council system, whereas middle-sized and small North American cities tend to use the [council–manager system](/wiki/Council%E2%80%93manager_government "Council–manager government").[[3]](#cite_note-GIA-3) The system is also commonly in place in Asian countries.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") uses a form that resembles the strong-mayor variant. [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") also uses the strong-mayor model system.

The mayor–council government system is also used in some Asian countries.

Mayors in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") are directly elected, holding significant power, with the directly elected council (assembly) providing a check and balance, operating under central government oversight according to the Japanese [Local Autonomy Act](/wiki/Local_Autonomy_Act "Local Autonomy Act") (UAL). While the local government has similarities to a strong mayor–council system, and the mayor has veto powers, there are also aspects of the parliamentary form of government; the local assembly can issue a vote of no confidence. If the vote is passed, the mayor will dissolve the assembly and the people will vote in a new one. If this happens a second time and passes, the mayor is removed.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan")'s [administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Taiwan "Administrative divisions of Taiwan") include six [special municipalities](/wiki/Special_municipality_(Taiwan) "Special municipality (Taiwan)"), including the capital [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei"), which use a mayor-council form of government; [counties](/wiki/County_(Taiwan) "County (Taiwan)") use a similar system with a chief executive called a [magistrate](/wiki/List_of_magistrates_and_mayors_in_Taiwan "List of magistrates and mayors in Taiwan").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mayor%E2%80%93council_government&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: See also")]

* [City commission government](/wiki/City_commission_government "City commission government")
* [Town meeting](/wiki/Town_meeting "Town meeting")
* *[Strong Mayors, Building Homes Act](/wiki/Strong_Mayors,_Building_Homes_Act "Strong Mayors, Building Homes Act")*
